utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Lukas. How are you today?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Hello James. I am good, thank you. And you?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: I'm very well, thank you. Right then, how was your week?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It was... okay. I have very much work this week.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: You had a lot of work this week. Yes, tell me about it.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes. We have a new project at my company. I must a new system... how you say... entwerfen?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Design. You must design a new system.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, design. I must design a new system for the... the water in a building.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: The plumbing system? Or the water supply?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, the water supply. For a new office building in Munich.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That sounds interesting. Is it a big building?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, very big. Twelve... Stockwerke... floors?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Twelve floors, yes, exactly right. That is a big project.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes. And every day I must go to the office now. No more home office.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Ah, so you have to commute every day now. How do you get to work?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I go with the car. It takes thirty minutes.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Interesting. Now, you said 'I go with the car'. In English we say 'I go by car' or 'I drive'. We'll talk more about that today, actually.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah, by car. Not with. Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. And that connects perfectly to our lesson today. We're going to talk about getting around the city.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Good. This is useful for me because sometimes I must also go to meetings in the city.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. So today we'll learn transport vocabulary, how to ask for and give directions, and then we'll practise with a map exercise.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay, sounds good.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right then. Before we start, did you do the homework from last week? The reading exercise?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, I did it. It was about shopping, yes? I have some questions.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good, we can look at those at the end if we have time. Let's get started with today's topic.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: So, Lukas, think about your city, Munich. How do people get around? What types of transport do they use?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Hmm. Bus, train, the... U-Bahn... underground?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, we say underground or the tube in London. In general English, you can also say subway or metro. What else?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Car, bicycle, and... zu Fuss... on foot?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: On foot, yes. Or we can say walk. 'I walk to work.' And one more important one - taxi.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah yes, taxi is same in German.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly, it's the same word. And we also say tram - do you have trams in Munich?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes! Strassenbahn. Tram, yes.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. So let me write our list. Bus, train, tram, underground, taxi, car, bicycle, and on foot. Can you read those back to me?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Bus, train, tram, underground, taxi, car, bicycle, on foot.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. Good pronunciation on all of those. Now, let's move on to how we use these words in sentences.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right. The key question is: 'How do you get to work?' Can you repeat that?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: How do you get to work?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Now, to answer, we use different verbs depending on the transport. With bus, train, tram, taxi, and underground, we say 'I take'. I take the bus. I take the train.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I take the bus. Like... nehmen?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly like nehmen, yes. And with car, we say 'I drive'. With bicycle, 'I ride' or 'I cycle'. And for walking, 'I walk'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Wait, let me write this down.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Of course, take your time.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. So, take for bus and train. Drive for car. Ride for bicycle. Walk for... walking.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. Now there's another way to say it. You can use 'go by'. I go by bus. I go by train. I go by car. But never 'go by foot' - it's 'go on foot'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Go by bus, go by train, but go on foot. Why is foot different?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good question. It's just an English expression, I'm afraid. 'On foot' is a fixed phrase. You just have to remember it.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Hmm. Okay. In German we say 'zu Fuss' which is also different from the transport ones. So maybe it is similar.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's a great observation, actually. Now, let's practise. Lukas, how do you get to work?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I go by car. I drive.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very good. And how does your wife get to work?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: She take the underground.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Almost. She takes - with an S. He takes, she takes, it takes. Remember the third person S?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah yes. She takes the underground. I always forget the S.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: It's very common at this stage. You'll get used to it. Now, how about your colleagues? How do they get to work?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Some take the bus. Some go by bicycle. One colleague, he drive very fast to work.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: He drives - with the S again. He drives very fast.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: He drives. Yes. He drives very fast. I think it is dangerous.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good sentence. Now let me ask you about last week. How did you get to work last Monday?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Last Monday I take the train because my car was... kaputt.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good try. But 'last Monday' is in the past. So we need past tense. Not 'I take' but 'I took'. I took the train.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I took the train. Take, took. Like German nehmen, nahm?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly, it's an irregular verb. Take, took, taken. And 'kaputt' - in English we say 'broken'. My car was broken. Or 'My car had broken down.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Broken down. Okay. Last Monday I took the train because my car was broken.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. Perfect sentence. Now, let's practise a few more. What about yesterday? How did you get to work yesterday?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yesterday I take... no. Yesterday I took the car. No wait, yesterday drove I to work.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good that you corrected 'take' to the past tense. But watch the word order. You said 'yesterday drove I'. In English, it's always subject before verb. 'Yesterday I drove to work.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah. Yesterday I drove to work. In German we say 'Gestern fuhr ich zur Arbeit' - the verb comes second and pushes the subject after.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. That's the V2 rule in German, isn't it? The verb must be in the second position. But in English, the subject almost always comes before the verb, no matter what comes first in the sentence.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: This is very hard for me. I always want to put the verb second.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: I understand. It's one of the biggest differences between German and English. We'll keep practising. Let me give you another one. What about this morning?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: This morning... this morning I drove to work. And then I walked to the office from the parking.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very good! We say 'car park' in British English, or 'parking lot'. Not just 'parking' on its own. But your sentence structure was perfect that time.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Car park. Okay. In German we say Parkplatz. So parking place... car park.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right then. Let's do a quick exercise. I'll say a time expression and you tell me how you got to work. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ready.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Last Friday.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Last Friday I go by train.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Past tense, Lukas. Last Friday I...?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Last Friday I went by train.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Well done. Go, went, gone. Next one: every morning.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Every morning I drive to work.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good, present tense because it's a routine. And: tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Tomorrow I will take the bus because my car is at the... Werkstatt.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: The mechanic or the garage. Tomorrow I will take the bus because my car is at the garage. Lovely sentence.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Garage. Like the French word. Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Now one more: last week.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Last week I go by train every day.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Remember, 'last week' needs past tense.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah. Last week I went by train every day.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. You're getting the hang of it. Now, there's an important difference I want to highlight. In English, we don't say 'I go with the bus'. We say 'I go by bus' or 'I take the bus'. 'With' is for people. 'I go with my friend.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah, because in German we say 'mit dem Bus' - with the bus. So it is a false... translation?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: A direct translation, yes. It's what we call a transfer error from German. 'By' for transport, 'with' for people.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Wait, let me write this down. By for transport, with for people.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. Now I want to also practise the question 'How long does it take?' This is very useful. How long does it take to get to work?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It takes thirty minutes with the car.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Almost. It takes thirty minutes by car. Remember - by for transport.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: By car. It takes thirty minutes by car. And by train it takes forty-five minutes.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very good. And how long does it take to walk to the nearest shop from your house?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It takes... five minutes on foot.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. On foot. Well remembered.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: James, can I ask something? The bus go every ten minutes in Munich. Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Almost. 'The bus goes every ten minutes.' The bus is 'it', so we need the S. The bus goes.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The bus goes. The train goes. Ah, the third person S again.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. You'll get it. It just takes practice. Right then, let's move on to directions vocabulary. This is a very practical topic.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Good, yes. I need this. Sometimes in meetings people explain me how to find the building and I don't understand.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. By the way, 'people explain to me' - we need 'to' before 'me' with the verb explain.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: People explain to me. Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Now, let me introduce the first set of direction words. These are the basic ones. Left, right, and straight. Can you repeat? Left.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Left.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Straight. Go straight.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Straight. Go straight.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Now with the verb 'turn'. Turn left. Turn right.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Turn left. Turn right.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: And we often say 'go straight on' or 'go straight ahead'. They mean the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Go straight on. Go straight ahead. What is the difference?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: No real difference. Both mean continue in the same direction without turning. I personally use 'go straight on' more often.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. Like geradeaus in German.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Now let's add some more words. Cross the street. Do you know 'cross'?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Cross... like ueberqueren? To go to the other side?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, exactly. Cross the street means go to the other side of the street.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Cross the street. Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Now, where do you cross the street? At the traffic light. Or at the... do you know what a pedestrian crossing is?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Traffic light is... Ampel... and pedestrian crossing is... Zebrastreifen?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: We actually do say 'zebra crossing' in British English! Because of the black and white stripes, like a zebra.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Oh! Like in German also! That is funny. Zebrastreifen - zebra stripes.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. So, cross the street at the traffic light. Cross the street at the zebra crossing. Now let's learn some position words.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Position words?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes. These tell you where something is in relation to something else. Next to, opposite, between, on the corner.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Next to... neben. Opposite is... gegenueber?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes. The bank is next to the supermarket. The hotel is opposite the train station. Can you repeat those?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The bank is next to the supermarket. The hotel is opposite the train station.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. And 'between'. The cafe is between the bank and the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The cafe is between the bank and the hospital. Like zwischen.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. And 'on the corner'. The shop is on the corner.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: On the corner. An der Ecke. The shop is on the corner.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. Now let's add 'at the end of the street' and 'at the beginning of the street'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: At the end of the street. At the beginning of the street.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. And one more: 'past'. Go past the bank. It means you walk by it, you don't stop.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Go past the bank. Vorbei. Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Now let me see if you can put some of these together. Can you describe where your office is?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: My office is... next to a big... Parkhaus... car park. And it is opposite a restaurant.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very good! You used 'next to' and 'opposite' perfectly.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: And the office is between the car park and a... Apotheke. How you say Apotheke?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: A pharmacy. Or in British English, we also say chemist.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Pharmacy. Okay. The office is between the car park and the pharmacy.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. That was really good, Lukas. Now, speaking of train stations, I know Munich has a very good train system, doesn't it?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes. The... Fahrplan... the drive plan... how do you say? The plan for when the trains come?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: The timetable. Or the schedule.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Timetable. Yes. The timetable is very good in Munich. The trains are always on time.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Ha! Well, in Britain, I'm afraid it's a bit different. Our trains are often late.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Really? I hear this before. Is it really so bad?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Sometimes, yes. We like to complain about the trains. It's almost a national hobby.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ha! In Germany also we complain about the Deutsche Bahn. But I think in Germany it is better than in Britain, no?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: I think you're probably right, yes. German trains have a good reputation. But let's get back to our directions vocabulary. We have a lot to cover today.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, sorry.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: No need to apologise. It's good to have these conversations. They're natural English practice. Now, let me teach you some more useful phrases.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: When you don't know where something is, you ask: 'Excuse me, where is the...?' Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Excuse me, vhere is the...?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good, but be careful with the W sound. Not 'vhere' - 'where'. In English, W is different from V. Where. Try again.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Where. Where is the...?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Much better. Where, not vhere. I know in German, W makes a V sound, but in English it's different.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, this is always difficult for me. W is like... you make your lips round?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Round your lips like you're going to whistle. Where. What. When. Why.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Where. What. When. Why. Okay, I will try to remember.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. So: 'Excuse me, where is the train station?' 'Excuse me, where is the nearest bank?' The word 'nearest' means the closest one.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Nearest. The closest. Excuse me, where is the nearest supermarket?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. There's also a more polite version: 'Excuse me, could you tell me where the train station is?' But at A1 level, 'where is the...' is absolutely fine.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay, I use the simple one for now.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good idea. Now, when someone gives you directions, they might say several things. Let me give you an example. 'Go straight on. Turn left at the traffic light. The bank is on your right.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Go straight on. Turn left at the traffic light. The bank is on your right. Okay, I understand.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Notice we say 'on your right' not 'on right'. You need the 'your'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: On your right, on your left. Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Now, another useful phrase: 'It's on the left-hand side' or 'It's on the right-hand side'. This is very common in giving directions.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Left-hand side. Right-hand side. Why do we say 'hand'?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: It's just a traditional expression. Think of it as the side where your left hand is. It's a fixed phrase.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. The bank is on the left-hand side.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. Now, let's practise some more direction sequences. I'll give you directions and you tell me where I end up. Imagine a simple street.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay, I will try.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Go straight on. Take the first turning on the left. Go past the pharmacy. The building is on your right. What building am I going to?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Hmm, I don't know which building, but I understand the directions. Go straight, then turn left, go past the pharmacy, it is on the right.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good, you understood perfectly. It was a trick question - I didn't name the building. But you followed the directions well.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah, okay. You test me.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Now, some useful words for distance. 'It's very close' - meaning near. 'It's quite far' - meaning not near. And 'It's about five minutes' walk' - meaning you walk for five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Five minutes' walk. Like in German, fuenf Minuten zu Fuss. It's about five minutes walk.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. You can also say 'It's a five-minute walk' or 'It's a ten-minute drive'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: A five-minute walk. A ten-minute drive. Wait, why is it not five-minutes with S?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent question. When we use it as an adjective before the noun, we drop the S. 'A five-minute walk' but 'It takes five minutes'. It's the same as 'a two-year-old child' not 'a two-years-old child'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah, that is a rule? Interesting. Let me write this down.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, take your time.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I have it. No S when it is before the noun.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. Now, let me also teach you what to say if you don't understand the directions. You can say: 'Sorry, could you repeat that?' or 'Sorry, could you speak more slowly?'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Sorry, could you repeat that? Sorry, could you speak more slowly? These are very useful.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: And if you're completely lost: 'I'm sorry, I'm lost. Could you help me?'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I'm lost. Could you help me? Yes. I become nervous when I am lost in a new city.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Ah, careful with that one. You said 'I become nervous'. In English, 'become' means to change into something. Here, you want to say 'I get nervous'. 'I get nervous when I am lost.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Get nervous? But in German, 'bekommen' is 'to get'. Not 'to become'. This is very confusing.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes! This is a very famous false friend between English and German. German 'bekommen' means 'to get' or 'to receive' in English. But English 'become' means 'werden' - to change into something.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: So when I want to say I become nervous, I should say I get nervous. Because bekommen is get.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly right. A classic trap for German speakers. 'I get nervous.' Not 'I become nervous' - unless you mean you are permanently transforming into a nervous person.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ha! No, I am not permanently nervous. I get nervous. Okay. Let me write this. Become is not bekommen.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Right then, let's move on to practising giving and following directions. This is where it gets fun.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay, I am ready.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: I'm going to give you directions, and you need to repeat them back to me. Think of it as if someone is telling you on the phone and you want to make sure you understood. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Go straight on for two hundred metres. Turn right at the traffic light. Go past the supermarket. The hospital is on your left.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I go straight on for two hundred metres. Then turn I right at the traffic light.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Stop there. 'Then turn I right' - remember the word order. Not 'turn I' but 'I turn'. Then I turn right.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah, the word order again. Then I turn right at the traffic light. I go past the supermarket. The hospital is on my left.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. Perfect correction. Did you notice you changed 'your left' to 'my left'? That was exactly right because now you're talking about yourself.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, because I am the one walking. So it is my left, not your left.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Very logical thinking. Let's do another one. Leave the train station. Turn left. Walk for about three minutes. Cross the street at the zebra crossing. The hotel is opposite the park.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I leave the train station. I turn left. I walk for about three minutes. I cross the street at the zebra crossing. The hotel is opposite the park.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Brilliant. Not a single mistake. Well done, Lukas.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Thank you. It is easier when I think about the subject first.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's a great strategy. Always think: subject, then verb. Now, let's switch roles. I'll be a tourist and I'll ask you for directions. You give me directions. Let's say I want to go to the supermarket.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I will try.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excuse me, where is the nearest supermarket?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The supermarket? Yes. First must you go straight on.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Almost. 'First must you' - that's the German word order again. In English: 'First you must go straight on.' Or even better: 'First, go straight on.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: First, go straight on. Ah yes. I did the same mistake again. First you must go straight on. Then turn left at the... the corner.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. And then?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Then go past the bank and the supermarket is on the... on your right.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very good. How far is it?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It is about a five-minute walk.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent! You used 'a five-minute walk' correctly. Well done.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I remember the rule - no S before the noun.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. Let's try another one. I'm a tourist again. Excuse me, where is the hospital?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The hospital. Go straight on. At the traffic light, turn right. Then... after the traffic light you must go... hmm... still straight?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: You can say 'continue straight on' or 'keep going straight'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Continue straight on. Okay. Continue straight on and the hospital is at the end of the street on the left-hand side.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Wonderful. A very clear set of directions. I would definitely find the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Good. I like this exercise. It is very practical.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: It is. Now, let's try one where you're the tourist this time. You ask me for directions.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Excuse me, where is the nearest bank?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: The bank? Yes. Go straight on for about fifty metres. Turn right at the first street. Walk past the cafe. The bank is next to the pharmacy on your left.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I go straight on for fifty metres. I turn right at the first street. I walk past the cafe. And the bank is next to the pharmacy on my left.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect repetition. And if I said it too fast, what would you say?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Sorry, could you speak more slowly?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Now let's do one more. You be the tourist and ask for the train station.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Excuse me, vhere is the train station?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Remember the W sound. Where, not vhere. Try again.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Where. Excuse me, where is the train station?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. The train station is quite far from here. It's about a fifteen-minute walk. Go straight on. At the second traffic light, turn left. Go past the park. Cross the big road. You'll see the train station in front of you.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I go straight on. At the second traffic light I turn left. I go past the park. I cross the big road. And then I see... I will see the train station.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: You can say 'I will see it' or just 'I can see it in front of me'. Both are fine.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: In front of me. Is it far?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good question to ask! It's about a fifteen-minute walk. Or you can take the bus - two stops.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Two stops. Like two Haltestellen. I take the bus. It is faster.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. 'Bus stop' is the English word for Haltestelle. 'It's two bus stops away.' Now then, let's add something. Sometimes directions use landmarks. A landmark is a building or place that is easy to see - like a church, a big shop, or a park.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Landmark. Like Wahrzeichen?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. So instead of saying 'turn left at the second street', you might say 'turn left at the church' or 'turn right when you see the big hotel'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: That is more natural, yes. In German also we say 'at the church turn left'.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Although notice the word order difference. You said 'at the church turn left' - that's the German way. In English, you can say that, but more naturally: 'Turn left at the church.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Turn left at the church. The important information comes first in English?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Often, yes. The action first, then the location. 'Turn left at the church.' 'Cross the street at the traffic light.' Let's practise with some more. Tell me how to get from the train station to a hotel.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. Leave the train station. Turn right. Go straight on. After the park... after the park turn I left.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Stop there. 'After the park turn I left.' What should it be?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: After the park... I turn left?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes! Or 'turn left after the park'. Either way, the subject 'I' comes before the verb 'turn'. Well done for correcting yourself.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah, I always make this mistake. The word order is so different from German.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: You're aware of it, which is the most important thing. Keep going with the directions to the hotel.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I turn left after the park. I go straight on for two minutes. I cross the street. The hotel is on the right-hand side, next to a restaurant.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent directions. Very clear, very structured. Very German, actually - precise and logical.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ha! I am an engineer. I like to be precise.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: It's a good quality for giving directions. Right then, let's move on to our map exercise. I'm going to share a simple city map with you.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay, I see it.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. So this map shows a small city centre. You can see the train station at the bottom, and there are various places marked: a supermarket, a bank, a hospital, a hotel, a park, a post office, some restaurants, and a museum.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, I can see. The train station is here, and the streets go... up and left and right.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Let's say you are standing at the train station, looking north - that's up on the map. There's a main street going straight ahead, which is called High Street. Can you see the supermarket?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, the supermarket is on the left side of the... High Street?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Now, I want to go to the supermarket from the train station. Can you give me directions?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. Leave the train station and go straight on High Street. The supermarket is on your left. It is next to the... is this a post office?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, it is. Well done. Simple and clear directions. How far is it?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It is maybe a three-minute walk.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good, with 'a three-minute walk'. You're remembering the rule. Now, a harder one. How do I get from the train station to the hospital?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: From the train station to the hospital. Okay. Leave the train station. Go straight on High Street. At the first traffic light, turn right. Go straight on. The hospital is... hmm... at the end of the street on the right-hand side.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very good. And where exactly is the hospital? Can you use a position word?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The hospital is between the bank and the... museum?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Looking at the map, the hospital is actually opposite the bank, and next to the museum. But good try using the position words.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Opposite the bank, next to the museum. Okay, I see now.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Now your turn to ask me. Choose a place on the map and ask me how to get there from the train station.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. Excuse me, where is the hotel?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: The hotel? Right. Leave the train station and go straight on up High Street. Take the second turning on the left. That's Park Road. Go along Park Road for about two minutes. The hotel is on your right, opposite the park.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. So I leave the train station, go straight on High Street. I take the second turning on the left, that is Park Road. I go along Park Road. The hotel is on my right, opposite the park.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect repetition. Now, let's do a role-play. You are a person who lives in this city. I'm a tourist. I'll ask you for help.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I am a local person. You are a tourist.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excuse me. I'm trying to find the bank. Could you help me?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, of course. Vhere... where are you now?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good, you corrected the W sound yourself. I'm at the supermarket.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: At the supermarket. Okay. Go to High Street. Turn right. Go straight on. At the traffic light, turn right again. The bank is on your left.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Is it far?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: No, it is very close. Maybe a five-minute walk.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Thank you very much. That's very helpful.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: You are welcome.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. 'You're welcome' - perfect response. Let's switch again. Now you're a tourist and I'm a local. Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Excuse me. I am lost. Could you help me? I vant to go to the museum.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Careful again - 'I want', not 'I vant'. The W sound.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I want. I want to go to the museum.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. The museum? Of course. Where are you now?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I am at the hotel.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right. Leave the hotel and turn right onto Park Road. At the end of Park Road, turn right onto High Street. Go straight on. Take the first turning on the right. The museum is on your right, next to the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Sorry, could you repeat that? You speak very fast.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Of course. Leave the hotel. Turn right. At the end of the road, turn right again. Go straight. Take the first right. The museum is on your right.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I leave the hotel. I turn right. At the end of the road, I turn right again. I go straight. I take the first right. And the museum is on my right. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: You're welcome. Have a nice day.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Thank you. You too.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good role-play. You did well. Now, one more scenario. This time, let's make it more challenging. You're at the park and you want to find a restaurant for lunch.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. Excuse me, I am looking for a restaurant. Is there a restaurant near here?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Great question format. Yes, there's a nice Italian restaurant on High Street. From the park, go to Park Road. Turn left and go to the end. Turn left onto High Street. The restaurant is on your right, between the supermarket and the post office.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I go to Park Road. I turn left. I go to the end. I turn left on High Street.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Almost. 'I turn left onto High Street.' We use 'onto' when you're moving to a different street.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Onto. I turn left onto High Street. The restaurant is on my right, between the supermarket and the post office.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. Very good. How long does it take?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It is about a ten-minute walk.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Can I also go by bus?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Hmm, I don't know. Maybe. I think there is a bus stop near the park. You can take the bus to High Street.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very natural answer. Good work, Lukas. Now, let me ask you something different. What do you do if you can not find the street?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I can the street not find? Hmm. I ask another person.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Okay, two things. First, you repeated 'I can the street not find' - that's the German word order. In German, the verb goes to the end with modal verbs: 'Ich kann die Strasse nicht finden.' But in English, it's 'I can't find the street.' The verb stays with the modal.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I can't find the street. Not 'I can the street not find'. The verb stays with can.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Can find, can't find. Must go, must not go. The verb always follows immediately after the modal in English.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: But in German the second verb goes to the end. This is so different. Wait, let me write this rule.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, take your time. It's an important rule.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. In English: modal plus verb together. I can find. I must go. Not like German where the verb is at the end.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. That's the key difference. Now, you also said 'I ask another person' - and that's a great answer. You could also use your phone for a map, or look for signs.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, of course. I use Google Maps usually. But sometimes in a building it does not work.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: True. Right then, let's do one more map exercise and then we'll move on to our review. This time, I want you to give me detailed directions from the museum to the train station.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: From the museum to the train station. Okay. Leave the museum. Turn left. Go straight on to the traffic light. At the traffic light, turn left onto High Street. Go straight on. High Street goes... directly to the train station. The train station is at the end of High Street.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Very good. How long is the walk?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It is about a ten-minute walk. Or you can take the bus, maybe two stops.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. And if I'm coming from the hospital, can you guide me to the park?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: From the hospital. Leave the hospital and turn left. Go to High Street and turn left. Go straight on. Take the second turning on the left. That is Park Road. Go along Park Road. The park is at the end on the right.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Brilliant directions. Very clear, very organized. You're doing really well with this.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Thank you. It helps to look at the map.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Absolutely. Real maps make it much easier to practise. Now, let's try one more where you need to describe a more complicated route. How do I get from the post office to the hotel?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The post office to the hotel. Okay. Leave the post office. Turn right onto High Street. Go straight on. In the morning take I... no. I walk along High Street. I take the second turning on the left onto Park Road. The hotel is on my right, opposite the park.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good - you caught yourself with the word order there. 'In the morning take I' - you stopped and corrected to 'I walk along'. What were you trying to say?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I was... confused. I wanted to say 'I walk along High Street' but the German came into my head first.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's completely normal. The fact that you caught it and corrected yourself is excellent progress. That shows real awareness.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes but it is frustrating. I know the rule but when I speak fast, I make the mistake.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's how language learning works. First you learn the rule, then you make mistakes, then you catch the mistakes, and finally it becomes automatic. You're at stage three - catching your own mistakes. That's very good for A1.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Stage three. Okay, that is good to know. How many stages are there?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Four. Learn, make mistakes, catch mistakes, automatic. You're doing well.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I will practise more.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. And the weather in Britain doesn't help with directions either. You know, half the time you're just trying to find somewhere dry.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ha! Yes, I was in London one time and it was raining every day. I was always lost and wet.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That sounds about right for London. Right then, let's move on to our review. We have about ten minutes left and I want to make sure everything is clear.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, good.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Okay, let's start with vocabulary. Can you name all the types of transport we learned today?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Bus, train, tram, underground, taxi, car, bicycle, on foot.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. All eight. Now, which verb do we use with bus and train?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Take. I take the bus. I take the train.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. And with car?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Drive. I drive to work.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: And the alternative structure? I go...?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I go by bus. I go by train. I go by car. But I go on foot.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. On foot is the exception. Well remembered. Now, direction words. Give me all the ones you can remember.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Left, right, straight on, turn, cross the street, traffic light, corner, next to, opposite, between, past, at the end of the street.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent memory. You got them all. Now, I'm going to say some sentences and I want you to tell me if they're correct or not. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, ready.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: I go with the bus to work.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Wrong. I go by bus to work. Or I take the bus to work.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Next: Yesterday I went by train.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Next: Then turned I right at the traffic light.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Wrong. Then I turned right at the traffic light.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. The V2 word order trap. You spotted it. Next: The hospital is on your right-hand side.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Next: She take the underground every day.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Wrong. She takes the underground every day. The third person S.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Well done. Next: I can not the way find.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Wrong. I can not find the way. Or I can't find the way. The verb stays with can.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Brilliant. You remembered the modal verb rule. One more: It's a ten-minutes walk.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Wrong. It's a ten-minute walk. No S before the noun.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: All correct. Very well done. You've really absorbed the rules today.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Thank you. I try to learn the rules. As an engineer, I like rules.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Ha! Well, English has rules, but it also has a lot of exceptions. We'll discover those as we go. Now, let's do one final speaking exercise. I want you to imagine you are on the phone with a colleague who is coming to visit your office for the first time. Give them directions from the train station to your office.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. Hello, welcome to Munich. You are at the train station?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, I've just arrived. How do I get to your office?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. Leave the train station from the main exit. Go straight on. You will see a big street. Cross the street at the traffic light.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good so far. Then what?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Then turn right. Go straight on for about... two hundred metres. You will see a big car park on the left. My office is next to the car park.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: How long will it take?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: It is about a ten-minute walk from the train station. Or you can take a taxi. It is faster.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: And if I get lost?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: If you get lost, call me and I will... I will come to find you. Or you can ask someone. People in Munich are friendly.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That was really good, Lukas. Very natural conversation. I noticed you used 'get lost' correctly this time - not 'become lost'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes! Because get is not become. I remember.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent. Now, let me quickly go over the main mistakes from today that you should be careful with.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, please. I want to know what I must practise.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right. Number one: word order. In English, the subject comes before the verb, even when the sentence starts with a time expression or a place. Not 'yesterday went I' but 'yesterday I went'. Not 'then turn I' but 'then I turn'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes. Subject before verb. Always.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Number two: with modal verbs like can, must, should - the main verb comes right after. Not 'I can the street not find' but 'I can't find the street'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Modal verb plus main verb together. Not at the end.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Number three: use 'by' for transport, not 'with'. By bus, by train, by car. But on foot.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: By for transport. With for people. On foot.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Number four: third person S. He takes, she drives, the bus goes. Don't forget the S.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: The S. He takes. She drives. It goes.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: And number five: past tense with time expressions. 'Yesterday I took', not 'yesterday I take'. 'Last week I went', not 'last week I go'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Past tense for past time. Yesterday I took. Last week I went.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. And remember the false friend: 'become' does not mean 'bekommen'. 'Get' is the word you want. I get nervous, not I become nervous.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Get, not become. Yes. I have this in my notes.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: And the W sound. Where, what, when, why, want. Not vhere, vhat, vhen, vhy, vant. Round your lips.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Where. What. When. Why. Want. I will practise in front of the mirror.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's actually a great idea. Practising in front of a mirror helps with pronunciation. Right then, let me give you your homework for next week.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: First, I want you to write directions from your home to three different places. Your office, the nearest supermarket, and the nearest train station.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Three sets of directions. From my home to the office, to the supermarket, and to the train station. Okay.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Use the vocabulary we learned today. Include distance information. 'It's a five-minute walk' or 'It's about two kilometres'.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: With distance. Okay. And should I use the position words? Next to, opposite, between?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely. Use as many direction words and position words as you can. The more you practise, the more natural it becomes.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. I will do it.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Second, I want you to practise the W sound. Make a list of ten words that start with W and say them out loud every day.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ten W words. Where, what, when, why, want, walk, work, water, weather, wait.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent, you already have ten. Say those every morning. And third, I've emailed you a worksheet with a map. Fill in the blanks with the correct prepositions and direction words.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay. The worksheet with the map. I will do it this weekend.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Don't leave it until Sunday evening.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ha! You know me too well. I always do homework on Sunday evening.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: I know the type. Engineers are good at deadlines but often work right up to them.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, that is true. But I will try to do it on Saturday.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good. Now, do you have any questions about today's lesson?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Yes, one question. You said 'take the first turning on the left'. What is 'turning'? Is it different from 'turn left'?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good question. A 'turning' is a noun - it means a place where you can turn. A side street. 'Take the first turning on the left' means 'turn left at the first side street'. 'Turn left' is the verb - the action.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Ah, so turning is the street, and turn is what I do. The first turning on the left is the first street on the left where I can turn.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly right. The first turning, the second turning, the third turning. It's very common in British English.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Okay, I understand now. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Any other questions?

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Hmm. One more. I will go to Berlin next month for work. Is there something different about directions in English when you are in a big city?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Not really, the vocabulary is the same. But in a big city, people might mention landmarks more - 'turn left at the big church', 'go past the shopping centre'. And they might give you underground directions: 'Take the U2 to Alexanderplatz.'

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Take the U2 to Alexanderplatz. Yes, that is useful. But I think in Berlin I will speak German.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Ha! Fair enough. But if you meet English-speaking colleagues there, you'll be ready. Right, I think that's everything for today. Very good lesson, Lukas. You've learned a lot of practical vocabulary.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Thank you, James. It was a good lesson. I like the map exercise very much.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: You did very well. Remember to watch the word order - subject before verb. And practise those W sounds.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: I will. Where, what, when, why, want. Every morning.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good pronunciation there. Much better than at the start of the lesson. Have a good week, Lukas, and I'll see you next Thursday.

utterances.speaker: Lukas
utterances.text: Thank you, James. Have a good week. Goodbye.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Goodbye, Lukas. Take care.
